Party_Details.Name: Paillart Philippe Marcel Etienne

Facts.Text: The first plaintiff, Philippe Marcel Etienne Paillart, is the managing director of the second plaintiff, Sin Rong Investments Pte Ltd, an investment company incorporated in Singapore.
On 28 April 2005, the first plaintiff was duly voted onto the board of directors of the second defendant in accordance with cl 2(f) of the Agreement.

On 28 April 2005, the first and second plaintiffs, the first and second defendants, Geraghty and CTG entered into a written agreement to enable the second plaintiff to financially invest and participate in the business development of the second defendant.

In cl 5(b) of the Agreement, it was provided that as long as the second plaintiff held no less than 10% of the shares in the second defendant, the first plaintiff would be entitled to be appointed a director of the second defendant.

The machine capable of performing the function described at [2] above was fabricated in April 2005 after the plaintiffs contributed nearly $800,000; the first defendant and Geraghty contributed about $4,500 each.

The defendants alleged that the first plaintiff acted without the second defendant's or the directors' approval, in passing a directors' resolution dated 6 May 2005 to register a company in the Free Zone Establishment in the Sharjah Airport International Free Zone in the United Arab Emirates.

The defendants alleged that the first plaintiff acted fraudulently in signing a power of attorney in favour of the first plaintiff's son, Nicholas Paillart, as neither the second defendant nor the directors had at any point in time consented or authorised the execution of the Power of Attorney.

The defendants alleged that the first plaintiff acted without the second defendant's or the directors' approval, in passing a directors' resolution dated 6 May 2005 to register a company in the Free Zone Establishment in the Sharjah Airport International Free Zone in the United Arab Emirates.

The defendants alleged that the first plaintiff acted fraudulently in signing a power of attorney in favour of the first plaintiff's son, Nicholas Paillart, as neither the second defendant nor the directors had at any point in time consented or authorised the execution of the Power of Attorney.

As can be seen from the application form dated 4 May 2005, submitted by Geraghty, for a SAIF-Zone licence, it was Geraghty who had inserted the name of "Nicholas Philippe" as the manager of the proposed FZE company.

In yet another application form submitted by ESI, this time for registration in the SAIF Zone as a FZE company, Geraghty had again inserted the name "Nicholas Philippe" in the column for the manager of the FZE company.

Clause 3 of the Agreement states that once the agreement has been executed and all of the activities described in cl 2 have been completed, it is the parties' intention to invite a company called Green SA, incorporated in Samoa, identified by Philippe as a willing and capable investor, to invest US$1.00 million in ESI, subject to a separate agreement.

In the Letter, the defendants alleged that the first plaintiff had acted without the second defendant's or the directors' approval, in passing a directors' resolution dated 6 May 2005 to register a company in the Free Zone Establishment in the Sharjah Airport International Free Zone in the United Arab Emirates.
The first plaintiff's response to the Letter was that the allegations were false. He therefore refused to resign.
The first plaintiff thereafter launched separate defamation proceedings in the Subordinate Courts.

On 27 June 2005, the first defendant purported, by a notice of that date, to call for an extraordinary general meeting of the second defendant to be held on 14 July 2005 with the purpose of removing the first plaintiff from the board of the second defendant.
The proposed resolution had to be circulated to the first plaintiff. This was not done.
The first plaintiff protested on several grounds against the first defendant's attempt to remove him from the board of the second defendant.
The second defendant only had a sum of $25.75 in its favour in its bank account in October 2004.

Despite Art 105 of the memorandum and articles of association of the second defendant allowing a resolution to be passed by a majority vote, at the least the proposed resolution had to be circulated to the first plaintiff.

The first defendant also alleged that the plaintiffs were guilty of trying to 'steal' the intellectual property rights belonging to the second defendant because the second plaintiff had tried to pay the second defendant's patent lawyers, M/s Lawrence YD Ho & Associates Pte Ltd, the fees for extending the patent to countries beyond Singapore.
The intellectual property rights in that machine were estimated by parties to be worth about $5m to $7m. It is undisputed that the machine was the second defendant's only and most valuable asset.

The second plaintiff's cheque was rejected by M/s Lawrence Ho as they took the view that they were unable to accept a cheque from Sin Rong Investment Pte Ltd for an ESI patent unless there is a Director's resolution from ESI to authorize it.

The second plaintiff's cheque was rejected by M/s Lawrence Ho as they took the view that they were unable to accept a cheque from Sin Rong Investment Pte Ltd for an ESI patent unless there is a Director's resolution from ESI to authorize it.

The machine had been sold to Oceanic Shiphandlers ('OS'), an entity which the first defendant admitted was 'friendly', for 41,210.50 dirhams on 11 September 2005 as part of 'a deal ... made between [Geraghty] and [OS]'.
Geraghty had removed the machine from the warehouse (where it had been kept) on 13 September 2005.
Geraghty had resigned as a director of ESI on 12 September 2005.
Some day in between 14 November and 21 November 2005, he had appointed Geraghty to be the guardian of the machine.

The first defendant had attempted to purchase the machine from OS by asking OS for a quote for the machine on 27 December 2005 on behalf of his own company, Kaitiaki Pte Ltd, a company of which he and his wife were the only shareholders and directors.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, given the 2005-04-28 agreement and the events surrounding the 2005-06-27 notice and related documents, the first plaintiff could be removed from the board of Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd by the first defendant through the attempted extraordinary general meeting.
Party_Details.Rule: A director's continued appointment may depend on contractual terms between shareholders and the company, and removal procedures must comply with the company's constitutional documents and applicable statutory requirements, including proper notice and circulation of board and shareholder resolutions.
Party_Details.Application: The 2005-04-28 agreement recorded that Sin Rong Investments Pte Ltd would hold shares in Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd and that, while it held at least a specified percentage, Philippe Marcel Etienne Paillart would be entitled to be appointed a director. After this agreement, the first defendant issued a notice on 2005-06-27 to convene an extraordinary general meeting to remove him, relying on conduct including the 2005-05-06 resolution and power of attorney and the Free Zone Establishment arrangements. The directors' circular resolution to convene the extraordinary general meeting was not circulated to Philippe Marcel Etienne Paillart, although the company's articles allowed majority decisions. The machine and its intellectual property rights were treated as the company's only and most valuable asset, and the plaintiffs had contributed substantially to its development. These facts frame whether the attempted removal complied with the contractual and corporate procedures governing his position.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judge analysed whether the default judgment against the second defendant rendered the declaratory relief against the first defendant unnecessary, applying principles from Salijah bte Ab Latef v Mohd Irwan bin Abdullah Teo and authorities on declaratory judgments. He concluded that a real controversy remained between the plaintiffs and the first defendant and that the plaintiffs were entitled to pursue declaratory and injunctive relief despite the default judgment. The court considered the first defendant's conduct in defending the action and raising the res judicata argument only at closing submissions, and treated the argument as a strategic attempt to avoid costs. On the contractual issue, the judge construed clause 3 of the agreement as expressing only an intention to invite Green SA to invest, not an obligation on the first plaintiff to procure the investment, and held that any failure to secure Green SA's investment did not amount to a repudiatory breach. He further held that, even if there had been a repudiatory breach, the defendants had not accepted it and the agreement, including clause 5(b), remained operative. On the alleged gross misconduct, the judge evaluated the evidence regarding the 2005-05-06 resolution and power of attorney, the use of the title "President", the involvement of Nicholas Paillart, and the Free Zone Establishment applications. He accepted the first plaintiff's explanation that the documents required signatures of the other directors and that the wording "we the Directors" indicated that they were not intended to be effective with only his signature. The judge rejected the allegations of fraud and gross misconduct, applying the standard of proof for serious allegations as discussed in Tang Yoke Kheng v Lek Benedict. He found that the first defendant had not proved the alleged misconduct on a balance of probabilities. The judge also examined the events surrounding the machine and its sale to Oceanic Shiphandlers, including the appointment of Geraghty as guardian after his resignation as director, the sale price, and the first defendant's subsequent attempt to purchase the machine through Kaitiaki Pte Ltd. He concluded that the first defendant had acted dishonestly in relation to the machine and that this undermined the first defendant's complaints about the first plaintiff's conduct. On the Companies Act defence, the judge held that, since no misconduct had been established, there was no contravention of section 157, and in any event proper procedures were required for removal. Regarding the notice of the extraordinary general meeting, the judge found that the directors' circular resolution to convene the meeting had not been sent to the first plaintiff and, applying Polybuilding (S) Pte Ltd v Lim Heng Lee, held that a meeting of the majority without notice to the minority was ineffective, rendering the notice of the extraordinary general meeting defective. The judge rejected the argument that the plaintiffs should be denied equitable relief on the basis of unclean hands, reiterating his findings on the lack of proven misconduct by the first plaintiff and the adverse view of the first defendant's conduct. He therefore granted the plaintiffs a declaration that, for as long as the second plaintiff holds at least 10% of the shares of the second defendant, the first plaintiff is entitled to be appointed a director of the second defendant, and a permanent injunction restraining the first defendant from convening or holding meetings or taking steps to remove the first plaintiff as director or to curtail his powers, together with damages to be assessed, interest, liberty to apply, and costs.

Party_Details.Name: Sin Rong Investments Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: Sin Rong Investments Pte Ltd was an investment company incorporated in Singapore and was the second plaintiff in this action.

On 2005-04-28, Sin Rong Investments Pte Ltd entered into a written agreement with the first and second defendants, Michael John Geraghty and Century Trading Group to enable it to financially invest and participate in the business development of Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd.
On 2005-04-28, pursuant to clause 2 of the written agreement, Sin Rong Investments Pte Ltd became a shareholder holding 22.22% of the shares in Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd.

On 2005-04-28, clause 5(b) of the written agreement provided that as long as Sin Rong Investments Pte Ltd held no less than 10% of the shares in Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd, Philippe Marcel Etienne Paillart would be entitled to be appointed a director of Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd.

Sin Rong Investments Pte Ltd had contributed nearly S$800,000 towards the fabrication of a machine for converting waste products from oil or date palm plantations into organic fibrous material for Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Sin Rong Investments Pte Ltd's shareholding and investment under the 2005-04-28 agreement entitled it to maintain Philippe Marcel Etienne Paillart as a director of Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd despite the first defendant's attempt to remove him.
Party_Details.Rule: Where a shareholder's contractual rights include an entitlement to have a specified individual appointed or maintained as a director while a minimum shareholding is held, any attempt to alter that position must comply with the contractual terms and the company's constitutional and statutory procedures.
Party_Details.Application: Under the 2005-04-28 agreement, Sin Rong Investments Pte Ltd acquired 22.22% of the shares in Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd and the agreement recorded that, while it held at least 10% of the shares, Philippe Marcel Etienne Paillart would be entitled to be appointed a director. Sin Rong Investments Pte Ltd contributed substantial funds to develop the company's key machine and later attempted to support the company's patent protection. The first defendant's 2005-06-27 notice sought to remove Philippe Marcel Etienne Paillart from the board without altering Sin Rong Investments Pte Ltd's shareholding position. These facts frame whether the attempted removal was consistent with the contractual arrangement linked to Sin Rong Investments Pte Ltd's investment and shareholding.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judge treated Sin Rong Investments Pte Ltd's contractual and shareholding position as central to the relief sought. He interpreted clause 5(b) of the agreement as conferring a continuing entitlement, while the second plaintiff held at least 10% of the shares, for the first plaintiff to be appointed a director. He rejected the first defendant's argument that an alleged breach of clause 3 by the first plaintiff allowed the defendants to treat the agreement as at an end, holding that clause 3 did not impose an obligation to procure Green SA's investment and that, in any event, no repudiation had been accepted. The judge concluded that the agreement remained in force and that Sin Rong Investments Pte Ltd's shareholding and the linked director-entitlement clause continued to operate. He therefore granted a declaration in favour of both plaintiffs that, for as long as Sin Rong Investments Pte Ltd holds at least 10% of the shares of Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd, the first plaintiff is entitled to be appointed a director, and he issued a permanent injunction restraining the first defendant from taking steps to remove the first plaintiff or to curtail his powers as director.

Party_Details.Name: Eban Stuart Ashley

Facts.Text: The first defendant, Stuart Ashley Eban, is a director of Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd, a company incorporated in Singapore.
Prior to 28 April 2005, the board of directors of Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd comprised the first plaintiff, the first defendant and Michael John Geraghty.

On 28 April 2005, the first and second plaintiffs, the first and second defendants, Geraghty and CTG entered into a written agreement to enable the second plaintiff to financially invest and participate in the business development of the second defendant.
The first defendant and Geraghty contributed about $4,500 each towards the fabrication of the machine in April 2005.

The first defendant's solicitors sent a letter dated 24 June 2005 calling for the first plaintiff's resignation and alleging misconduct concerning a directors' resolution, a power of attorney, and the registration of a Free Zone Establishment company.

On 2005-06-27, the first defendant issued a notice purporting to call for an extraordinary general meeting of Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd to be held on 2005-07-14 with the purpose of removing Philippe Marcel Etienne Paillart from the board of Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd.

On 27 June 2005, the first defendant purported to call for an extraordinary general meeting to be held on 14 July 2005 for the purpose of removing the first plaintiff from the board of the second defendant.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the first defendant could, based on the 2005-04-28 agreement, the company's constitution and the conduct of the first plaintiff, validly initiate and rely on an extraordinary general meeting to remove the first plaintiff from the board of Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd.
Party_Details.Rule: A director seeking to remove another director must act within the framework of any relevant shareholder or investment agreement, comply with the company's memorandum and articles of association regarding board and shareholder decision-making, and observe statutory requirements for notice and procedure when convening meetings.
Party_Details.Application: The first defendant was a director of Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd and a party to the 2005-04-28 agreement under which Sin Rong Investments Pte Ltd acquired a shareholding and the first plaintiff's entitlement to a board position was linked to that shareholding. On 2005-06-24, the first defendant, through solicitors, sent a letter to the first plaintiff calling for his resignation and listing misconduct. On 2005-06-27, he issued a notice to convene an extraordinary general meeting on 2005-07-14 to remove the first plaintiff, relying on a directors' circular resolution that was not circulated to the first plaintiff, despite the company's articles addressing majority decisions. These steps, together with his later involvement in the appointment of Michael John Geraghty as guardian of the machine and the sale and attempted repurchase of the machine, formed the factual background against which the court assessed whether his attempt to remove the first plaintiff complied with the contractual and corporate procedures.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judge considered the first defendant's five main defences: alleged breach of the agreement by failure to secure Green SA's investment, alleged gross misconduct by the first plaintiff, alleged contravention of section 157 of the Companies Act, the propriety of convening the extraordinary general meeting, and the contention that equitable relief should be denied due to unclean hands. On the agreement, the judge held that clause 3 expressed only an intention to invite Green SA to invest and did not impose an obligation on the first plaintiff to procure the investment; thus, there was no repudiatory breach. He further held that, even if there had been such a breach, the defendants had not accepted any repudiation, so the agreement, including clause 5(b), remained in force. On the alleged gross misconduct, the judge examined the 2005-05-06 resolution and power of attorney, the use of the title "President", the wording "we the Directors", and the involvement of Nicholas Paillart. Applying the heightened scrutiny appropriate for serious allegations, he concluded that the first defendant had not proved fraud or gross misconduct on a balance of probabilities. The judge also reviewed the events concerning the machine, including the appointment of Geraghty as guardian after his resignation as director, the sale to Oceanic Shiphandlers at 41,210.50 dirhams, and the first defendant's subsequent attempt to purchase the machine through Kaitiaki Pte Ltd. He found that the first defendant had acted dishonestly in relation to the machine and that this undermined the first defendant's position in complaining about the first plaintiff's conduct. On the Companies Act defence, the judge held that, since no misconduct had been established, there was no breach of section 157, and in any event proper procedures were required for removal. Regarding the extraordinary general meeting, the judge found that the directors' circular resolution to convene the meeting had not been sent to the first plaintiff and, relying on Polybuilding (S) Pte Ltd v Lim Heng Lee, held that a meeting of the majority without notice to the minority was ineffective, rendering the notice of the extraordinary general meeting defective. The judge rejected the argument that the plaintiffs should be denied equitable relief due to unclean hands, noting both the failure to prove misconduct by the first plaintiff and the adverse findings about the first defendant's conduct. He also rejected the first defendant's argument that the action was misconceived due to the default judgment against the second defendant, holding that a real controversy remained and that the first defendant, having chosen to defend the action and raise numerous allegations, could not rely on res judicata to avoid the relief and costs consequences. The court therefore granted the declaratory and injunctive relief sought against the first defendant, together with damages to be assessed, interest, liberty to apply, and costs.

Party_Details.Name: Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: Before the plaintiffs became involved in its affairs, Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd had a sum of $25.75 in its favour in its bank account in October 2004.
Prior to 28 April 2005, Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd was wholly owned by Century Trading Group, a company incorporated in the Territory of the British Virgin Islands.
Prior to 28 April 2005, the board of directors of Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd comprised Philippe Marcel Etienne Paillart, Stuart Ashley Eban and Michael John Geraghty.
Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd's memorandum and articles of association included Article 105, which allowed a resolution to be passed by a majority vote.
The defendants' patent lawyers were M/s Lawrence YD Ho & Associates Pte Ltd in relation to the machine's intellectual property rights.

Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd was the sole beneficial owner of the intellectual property rights relating to a machine for converting waste products from oil or date palm plantations into organic fibrous material.

The intellectual property rights in the machine for converting waste products from oil or date palm plantations into organic fibrous material were estimated by the parties to be worth about $5m to $7m and were the second defendant's only and most valuable asset.

On 28 April 2005, the first and second plaintiffs, the first and second defendants, Geraghty and CTG entered into a written agreement to enable Sin Rong Investments Pte Ltd to financially invest and participate in the business development of Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd.
On 28 April 2005, pursuant to clause 2 of the written agreement, Sin Rong Investments Pte Ltd became a shareholder holding 22.22% of the shares of Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd.

On 28 April 2005, clause 3 of the written agreement stated that it was the parties' intention to invite Green SA, incorporated in Samoa and identified by Philippe as a willing and capable investor, to invest US$1.00 million in ESI, with the terms and conditions to be subject to a separate agreement between ESI and Green SA.

On 28 April 2005, clause 5(b) of the written agreement provided that as long as the second plaintiff held no less than 10% of the shares in the second defendant, the first plaintiff would be entitled to be appointed a director of the second defendant.
The machine was fabricated in April 2005 after the plaintiffs contributed nearly $800,000; the first defendant and Geraghty contributed about $4,500 each.
The machine capable of performing the described function was fabricated in April 2005.
On 4 May 2005, as can be seen from the application form submitted by Geraghty for a SAIF-Zone licence, Geraghty inserted the name of "Nicholas Philippe" as the manager of the proposed FZE company.

In yet another application form submitted by ESI, this time for registration in the SAIF Zone as a FZE company, Geraghty had again inserted the name "Nicholas Philippe" in the column for the manager of the FZE company.

A directors' resolution dated 6 May 2005 was issued under the second defendant's name, bearing the sole signature of the first plaintiff with the title of "President", relating to registration of a FZE company in the SAIF Zone in the UAE, full financial commitment by ESI, appointment of Nicholas Paillart as manager/negotiator, and execution of a power of attorney.

A power of attorney dated 6 May 2005 was issued under the second defendant's name in favour of Nicholas Paillart, with an opening paragraph stating "we the Directors of Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd" and bearing the signature of the first plaintiff with the title "President".

The first plaintiff had both documents notarised and certified and then had his son, Nicholas Paillart, attempt to submit the Resolution to meet a banking requirement that required a bona fide directors' resolution of ESI.
On 12 September 2005, Geraghty resigned as a director of ESI.
On 13 September 2005, Geraghty had removed the machine from the warehouse where it had been kept.
On 11 September 2005, the machine had been sold to Oceanic Shiphandlers for 41,210.50 dirhams as part of a deal made between Geraghty and OS.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, in light of the 2005-04-28 agreement, the company's constitutional documents, and the events concerning the machine and the Free Zone Establishment arrangements, Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd could be used as the vehicle for the first defendant's attempt to remove the first plaintiff from its board.
Party_Details.Rule: A company is bound by its constitutional documents and by shareholder or investment agreements to which it is a party, and actions taken in its name, including attempts to alter board composition or dispose of key assets, must comply with those instruments and applicable company law.
Party_Details.Application: Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd was wholly owned by Century Trading Group before the 2005-04-28 agreement and then became that agreement, under which Sin Rong Investments Pte Ltd acquired 22.22% of its shares and the first plaintiff's entitlement to a board position was linked to Sin Rong Investments Pte Ltd's shareholding. Its memorandum and articles, including Article 105, governed board decision-making. The company owned the intellectual property rights in the machine, which was treated as its only and most valuable asset, and was involved in Free Zone Establishment applications in the Sharjah Airport International Free Zone. Documents dated 2005-05-06, including a directors' resolution and a power of attorney, were issued under its name, and later the machine was sold to Oceanic Shiphandlers and removed from storage. Against this background, the first defendant used a directors' circular resolution and a notice of extraordinary general meeting in the company's name to attempt to remove the first plaintiff from the board, raising questions about whether those steps complied with the agreement and the company's constitution.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: Default judgment was entered against Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd on 2005-11-11 due to its failure to file a defence. The judge considered whether this default judgment rendered further declaratory relief unnecessary and concluded that it did not, because a real controversy remained between the plaintiffs and the first defendant and because the first defendant could have sought to set aside the default judgment or to carry on the company's defence but did not do so. The judge treated the company's position as governed by the 2005-04-28 agreement and its memorandum and articles, including Article 105. He held that the agreement, including clause 5(b), remained in force and that the company's use as a vehicle for the first defendant's attempt to remove the first plaintiff had to comply with those instruments. The judge found that the directors' circular resolution to convene the extraordinary general meeting had not been sent to the first plaintiff and, applying Polybuilding (S) Pte Ltd v Lim Heng Lee, held that a meeting of the majority without notice to the minority was ineffective, rendering the notice of extraordinary general meeting defective. He also noted the company's ownership of the machine and its intellectual property rights, the sale of the machine to Oceanic Shiphandlers, and the subsequent attempt by the first defendant to reacquire it through Kaitiaki Pte Ltd, and he regarded these events as relevant to assessing the conduct of those acting in the company's name. The court ultimately granted declaratory and injunctive relief that constrained the use of Earth Science Industries Pte Ltd as a vehicle for removing the first plaintiff from its board and ordered damages, interest, liberty to apply, and costs.
